perturbation.original_text: (a) “Affiliate ” means, with respect to any Person, any other Person that is directly or indirectly Controlling, Controlled by or under common Control with such Person, where “ Control ” and derivative terms mean the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a Person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) “Affiliate” means, with respect to any Person, any other Person that is directly or indirectly Controlling, Controlled by or under common Control with such Person. "Control" means only the possession of 50% or more of voting securities.
perturbation.explanation: The modification limits the definition of "Control" to only ownership of voting securities, contradicting the broader definition that includes 'by contract or otherwise,' creating ambiguity regarding affiliate status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Rule 12b-2 Definition of "Control"
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text restricts the definition of 'control' to only ownership of 50% or more of voting securities, which conflicts with the SEC's broader definition in Rule 12b-2 that includes 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o

perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines 'Control' broadly, including control exerted 'by contract or otherwise.' The changed text narrows this definition to 'only the possession of 50% or more of voting securities.' This contradicts the SEC's definition of control as defined in Rule 12b-2, which includes 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not contain the specific definition of control, the Law Explanation indicates that the modified text conflicts with the SEC's broader definition. The changed text therefore limits the scope of 'control,' creating a contradiction as it does not align with established legal definitions, thus falling under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: (d) “Person ” means any natural person, business, corporation , company, association, limited liability company, partners hip, limited partnership, limited liability partnership, joint venture, business enterprise, trust, governmental authority or other legal entity.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) “Person” means any natural person or corporation.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowing the definition of “Person” to only natural persons or corporations, the modified text excludes other business entities like LLCs, partnerships, and trusts, potentially affecting liability and obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition omits several entities included in the definition of 'person' as outlined in 1 U.S. Code § 1, which states 'In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise… the words “person” or “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals.'

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person"
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Person' compared to the original contract, excluding entities like LLCs, partnerships, and trusts. This contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1, which includes 'corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals' within the definition of 'person'. The scraped snippet 1 from 1 USC 1 supports this, stating that 'In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things'. By limiting the definition, the modified text creates a contradiction because it deviates from the legally recognized, broader definition of 'person' established in 1 U.S. Code § 1.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall (i) use the Ev aluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating, negotiating and consummating th e Transaction (or other legal, audit or compliance purposes permitted by this Agreement);
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall (i) use the Evaluation Material for any purpose they deem necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the permitted use of Evaluation Material from a defined purpose to "any purpose they deem necessary" contradicts the principle of confidentiality and restricted use. It creates uncertainty about protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the buyer to use the evaluation material for 'any purpose they deem necessary,' the modified text contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Misappropriation, as defined in the Act, includes unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret, which this modification permits.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing the buyer to use the evaluation material for "any purpose they deem necessary," directly contradicts the principles of trade secret protection, as it removes the restriction of use to evaluating, negotiating, and consummating the transaction or other permitted purposes. This broadens the scope to include potentially unauthorized uses, which is a key concern addressed by the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). The UTSA, as indicated in the provided Wikipedia snippet, aims to resolve inconsistencies in trade secret protection and provides definitions for 'trade secret' and 'trade secret misappropriation.' By allowing 'any purpose,' the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, as it permits uses beyond the originally intended and agreed-upon scope, which is a core aspect of protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that neither Buyer nor any of its Representatives will contact or communicate with any of the directors, officers, employees, customers, suppliers, distributors, licensees, licensors and clients of the Company, and other persons, including governmental agencies, having business relations with the Company, regarding the Company or the Transaction, in each case except to the extent such contacts and communications are (i) made in the ordinary course of business of Buyer or the applicable Representative s and are unrelated to the Transaction and the evaluation of the Transaction conducted by Buyer and its Representatives or (ii) approved in advance and in writing (including by email) by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that neither Buyer nor any of its Representatives will contact any of the Company’s directors, officers, or employees regarding the company or the transaction.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the restriction on contacting customers, suppliers, etc. This severely reduces the protection the target company has against potential damage from the buyer interfering with its business relationships during the evaluation phase.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tortious Interference with Contractual Relations (Common Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the restriction on contacting customers, suppliers, etc., the revised text increases the risk of tortious interference. While a confidentiality agreement doesn't directly prevent a tort claim, it provides evidence of the intended scope of permitted activities. Removing this restriction increases the likelihood that the buyer's actions could be interpreted as intentionally disrupting the company's business relationships, thereby increasing exposure to a tortious interference claim.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a significant restriction on who the buyer can contact, specifically excluding customers, suppliers, distributors, licensees, licensors, and clients, which were all protected in the original text. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change "severely reduces the protection the target company has against potential damage from the buyer interfering with its business relationships during the evaluation phase." The Law Explanation then states that this change "increases the risk of tortious interference" and "increases the likelihood that the buyer's actions could be interpreted as intentionally disrupting the company's business relationships, thereby increasing exposure to a tortious interference claim." While the provided law snippets are not helpful, the original contract language clearly protects the business from interference in a way that the changed language does not. Removing this protection introduces a serious legal contradiction, as it could increase liability exposure, aligning with the definition of misaligned terminology where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for a breach or a threatened breach of this Agreement and that each party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief, without the posting of a bond or other security, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that money damages shall be the sole remedy for any breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that money damages are the *sole* remedy, the revision eliminates the possibility of injunctive relief. This may not be legally permissible, particularly in cases where the damage from a breach is irreparable (such as leaking trade secrets).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 - Effect of Adequacy of Damages
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the general principles of contract law, specifically the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359, which states that specific performance or injunctive relief will not be ordered if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party's expectation interest. By making money damages the *sole* remedy, even where it is inadequate, the contract deviates from established legal principles.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for specific performance and injunctive relief, acknowledging that money damages may not always be sufficient. The changed text states that money damages are the *sole* remedy, thereby eliminating the possibility of injunctive relief, even when monetary compensation is inadequate. This contradicts the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359, which discusses specific performance or injunctive relief when damages are inadequate. While the provided snippets don't directly quote the Restatement, the Law Explanation asserts this contradiction based on established legal principles of contract law. The changed text fundamentally alters the remedies available, creating a legal contradiction. The absence of useful scraped snippets does not diminish the contradiction present based on the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, and all claims or causes of action (whether in contract, tort or otherwise) th at may be based upon, arise out of or relate to this Agreement, or the negotiation, execution or performance of this Agreement, shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of [New York]31 without regard to the principles of conflicts of laws in any jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of [Delaware], applying Delaware's conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law to Delaware and specifying the application of its conflict of law principles introduces uncertainty. This could lead to the application of laws less favorable to one party, undermining the original intent. New York law and Delaware law have different approaches to corporate governance and contract interpretation, impacting the outcome of disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles - Generally, Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the governing law and adding “applying Delaware's conflict of law principles,” the modified text can contradict the parties' original expectations if those principles would result in the application of a law other than Delaware law that significantly alters their contractual rights and obligations, potentially violating established choice of law principles as outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, which emphasizes upholding the parties' reasonable expectations and the fundamental policy of the chosen state.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from New York to Delaware and specifies that Delaware's conflict of law principles apply. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this could lead to the application of laws less favorable to one party and undermine the original intent. The Law Explanation says this change can contradict the parties' original expectations. The provided snippet from Wex Law Dictionary explains that conflict of laws arises when there's a difference between the laws of two jurisdictions connected to a case, affecting the outcome based on which jurisdiction's law is used. By explicitly stating Delaware's conflict of law principles apply, the contract introduces a potentially significant shift in legal interpretation and enforcement that could contradict the original intent, thus creating a misaligned terminology legal contradiction as defined.
